ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for sweet lassi specifically; A0FKP (fermented milk drink, sweetened) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for sweet lassi or sweetened yogurt beverage in available terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Sweet Lassi is approximately 250 ml (one large glass), equivalent to roughly 250 g, as commonly served in North Indian dhabas and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Sweet lassi values adapted from NIN/ICMR Nutritive Value of Indian Foods using yogurt (whole milk, sweetened) composition data. Added-sugar contribution estimated at approximately 8–10 g per 100 ml based on standard recipe proportions (2 parts yogurt, 1 part water, 1–2 tsp sugar per 100 ml). Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 170886) to validate protein, fat, and calcium figures. Saturated fat estimated from USDA whole-milk yogurt data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — yogurt and fermented dairy beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sweet lassi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 is estimated from the closest analogous food: sweetened whole-milk yogurt (GI ~36–57, mean ~48) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The protein and fat content of full-cream yogurt slow glucose absorption, yielding a low GI despite added sugar. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 28.0) / 100 = 13.4 (250 g serving × 11.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 28.0 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium (140 mg/100 g) and calcium (110 mg/100 g) content with low sodium (40 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Sweet lassi's favorable potassium-to-sodium ratio supports blood pressure regulation; however, individuals with hypertension should consume unsalted versions and monitor total caloric intake, as the added sugar contributes to overall energy load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added refined sugar contributing approximately 10.5 g sugar per 100 g, partially offset by low GI (~48) due to yogurt protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid standard sweet lassi due to its significant added sugar content; unsweetened or stevia-sweetened lassi is a preferable alternative, and portion size should be discussed with a registered dietitian.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g) from whole-milk yogurt and added sugar contributing to overall energy density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sweet lassi made with whole-milk yogurt provides moderate saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should opt for low-fat yogurt versions and limit added sugar to reduce atherogenic risk, while benefiting from the probiotic and potassium content.

lactose_intolerance.key_factor: Dairy-based beverage containing lactose from yogurt
lactose_intolerance.guidance: Individuals with lactose intolerance should avoid sweet lassi or substitute with lactose-free yogurt, as the yogurt base contains lactose despite partial fermentation reducing lactose content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet Lassi is a traditional North Indian yogurt-based beverage originating from the Punjab region of India. It is prepared by vigorously whisking or blending thick whole-milk yogurt (curd) with sugar, chilled water, and optional flavorings such as green cardamom, rose water, or saffron until a rich, frothy consistency is achieved. Traditionally prepared using a wooden hand-blender called a mathani, it is served in large earthen pots (kulhads) and often topped with a dollop of malai (clotted cream) or unsalted white butter. The texture is creamy and smooth with a characteristic foam layer; the flavor is sweet, mildly tangy from the yogurt, and aromatic when cardamom or rose water is added. Per 100 g, sweet lassi provides approximately 98 kcal, 3.2 g protein, 4.5 g fat, and 11.2 g carbohydrates with 10.5 g sugar. It is a good source of calcium (110 mg), potassium (140 mg), riboflavin (0.15 mg), and vitamin B12 (0.4 mcg). Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 48 (low), as the protein and fat from yogurt slow glucose absorption despite the added sugar. Clinically, sweet lassi is suitable for most healthy adults as a cooling summer beverage and digestive aid; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake due to added sugar, and those with lactose intolerance should avoid it. Its probiotic content from live yogurt cultures supports gut health and may have anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis.

Is sweet lassi good for diabetes?

meal_context: accompaniment to spicy Punjabi meals such as dal makhani and paratha
snack or refreshment at any time of day

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from NIN/ICMR Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy whole-milk yogurt data. No exact sweet lassi entry exists in any single approved source; values represent a well-supported adaptation based on standard recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for sweetened whole-milk yogurt. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, cholesterol, and water content are estimated from USDA whole-milk yogurt data.
notes: 1. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g per 100 g: sweet lassi is a liquid dairy beverage with no plant fibre-contributing ingredients in its base formulation; this is confirmed zero per NIN/ICMR yogurt composition data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170886). 2. Trans fat is 0.0 g per 100 g: naturally occurring trans fats from ruminant dairy (vaccenic acid) are present at trace levels below reporting threshold; confirmed negligible per USDA data. 3. Saturated fat (2.8 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.1 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1 g), cholesterol_mg (17.0 mg), and water_g (80.0 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy whole-milk plain yogurt (FDC ID 170886) adjusted for dilution with water and added sugar in standard sweet lassi recipe. 4. The portions array in the input contained four identical duplicate entries ('1 serving (250ml)', 250 g); deduplicated to a single canonical serving of 250 g. 5. The input food_group was 'Snacks'; reclassified to 'Beverages' as sweet lassi is a liquid yogurt-based drink. 6. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for sweet lassi was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 7. FoodEx2 code A0FKP used as closest defensible category anchor; no dedicated FoodEx2 code for sweet lassi exists. 8. Glycemic load per serving = (48 × 28.0) / 100 = 13.4, based on 250 g serving providing 28.0 g carbohydrate. 9. Q8 fix (iteration 3): the previous draft contained 'மீठी लस्सी' (mixed-script error) in aliases_by_language.hindi; corrected to 'मीठी लस्सी' and verified all aliases_by_language string values appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
